findings.title: audit_logs created with ON DELETE SET NULL — immutability trigger added 31 days later, leaving a window where audit rows could be silently mutated
findings.code_evidence: ALTER TABLE "audit_logs" ADD CONSTRAINT "audit_logs_actorId_fkey" FOREIGN KEY ("actorId") REFERENCES "users"("id") ON DELETE SET NULL ON UPDATE CASCADE;
findings.description: At creation time (20260424), audit_logs.actorId FK is declared ON DELETE SET NULL. Any user deletion between migration 20260424111457 and the immutability trigger migration 20260525190000 (31 days later) silently issues an UPDATE on audit_logs rows, setting actorId to NULL. This mutates the audit record without triggering any protection. The schema comment and 20260525190000 migration both acknowledge that SET NULL on an immutable table is problematic — the FK was later changed to NO ACTION — but the 31-day window is unprotected. If any user was hard-deleted during that window in production, those audit rows were silently modified and the actor identity is permanently lost.
findings.root_cause: The immutability trigger and the FK ON DELETE behavior were designed together but deployed in two separate migrations 31 days apart, leaving the audit table mutable in the interim.
findings.impact: Any user deletion between 2026-04-24 and 2026-05-25 that had associated audit_logs rows permanently erased the actor identity in those rows; audit trail is no longer reliable for that period. Compliance and forensic audits may be affected.
findings.suggested_fix: Verify prod audit_logs for rows where actorId IS NULL and actorEmail (if added) is also NULL — these may be the result of the SET NULL window. Additionally, the original migration should have set ON DELETE RESTRICT or NO ACTION to protect immutability from the start. Document the window in a SECURITY_NOTE.
acceptance_criteria: No audit_logs row has actorId=NULL unless the user was deleted AFTER 2026-05-25 (when actorEmail snapshot was also captured)
Future audit_logs FK additions always use ON DELETE NO ACTION or RESTRICT
findings.verification_command: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM audit_logs WHERE "actorId" IS NULL AND "createdAt" < '2026-05-25';
findings.notes: The immutability trigger (20260525190000) itself correctly notes at line 95-104 that 'A SetNull on user deletion issues an UPDATE on audit_logs that the immutability trigger would reject', confirming this was a known design flaw fixed retroactively.

findings.title: time_entries.thirdPartyId ON DELETE CASCADE silently destroys billing/payroll records when a ThirdParty is deleted
findings.description: Time entries are billing and payroll records. When a ThirdParty row is deleted (soft-delete via isActive=false exists but hard-delete is not blocked), all associated time_entries rows are CASCADE deleted. This destroys historical billing/HR records without warning or audit trail. The sister constraint for user-linked time entries (time_entries.userId) is also ON DELETE CASCADE in schema.prisma (line 462), compounding the risk. For an HR management system in French local government, these records may be subject to retention obligations (up to 5 years under French labor law). There is no RESTRICT guard preventing ThirdParty deletion when time entries exist.
findings.root_cause: The FK was set to ON DELETE CASCADE following the same pattern as task_third_party_assignees (where CASCADE is correct for a join table), but time_entries are substantive financial/HR records that should survive their actor's deletion.
findings.impact: Deleting a third_party (intentionally or accidentally) irrecoverably destroys all associated time entries. These represent worked hours and may be legally required for payroll and billing purposes. No recovery path exists without a database backup.
findings.suggested_fix: Change ON DELETE CASCADE to ON DELETE RESTRICT on time_entries.thirdPartyId FK. If deletion of a ThirdParty with associated time entries must be allowed, implement a soft-delete pattern (ThirdParty.isActive already exists) and block hard-deletes via application logic or a BEFORE DELETE trigger that raises an exception when time_entries referencing that ThirdParty exist.
acceptance_criteria: DELETE FROM third_parties WHERE id = '...' fails with FK violation when time_entries reference that third_party
Integration test: create third_party + time_entry, attempt delete of third_party, expect error
findings.verification_command: SELECT tp.id, COUNT(te.id) as te_count FROM third_parties tp JOIN time_entries te ON te."thirdPartyId" = tp.id GROUP BY tp.id HAVING COUNT(te.id) > 0 LIMIT 5;
findings.notes: Contrast with project_snapshots which uses ON DELETE RESTRICT (DAT-007) specifically to preserve history. Same principle should apply to time_entries.

findings.title: project_third_party_members.allocation INTEGER has no CHECK constraint for 0-100 range — unlike project_members.allocation which was fixed in DAT-004
findings.code_evidence:
"allocation"    INTEGER,
  "assignedById"  TEXT NOT NULL,
  "createdAt"     TIMESTAMP(3) NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  CONSTRAINT "project_third_party_members_pkey" PRIMARY KEY ("id")
findings.description: project_third_party_members.allocation stores a percentage (0-100%) just like project_members.allocation. The DAT-003/DAT-004 business invariants migration (20260527120000) added CHECK ("allocation" BETWEEN 0 AND 100) for project_members but explicitly omitted project_third_party_members. This asymmetry means a buggy service path or direct SQL admin can insert allocation=-5 or allocation=150 for third-party members without any DB-level rejection. The schema comment for project_members reads '0-100%', implying the same semantic for both tables.
findings.root_cause: The DAT-004 migration added allocation range checks only for project_members but missed the parallel table project_third_party_members which has the same column with the same semantic.
findings.impact: Out-of-range allocation values for third-party members can corrupt workload calculations and capacity reports, leading to incorrect analytics. Applications may silently accept and display nonsensical values (e.g., -10% or 200% allocation).
findings.suggested_fix: Add: ALTER TABLE "project_third_party_members" ADD CONSTRAINT "project_third_party_members_allocation_ck" CHECK ("allocation" BETWEEN 0 AND 100);
acceptance_criteria: INSERT INTO project_third_party_members (..., allocation) VALUES (..., 101) fails with check_violation
INSERT INTO project_third_party_members (..., allocation) VALUES (..., -1) fails with check_violation
INSERT INTO project_third_party_members (..., allocation) VALUES (..., 50) succeeds
findings.verification_command: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM project_third_party_members WHERE allocation < 0 OR allocation > 100;
findings.notes: Verified by exhaustive grep: no migration in the entire migrations directory adds any CHECK constraint to project_third_party_members.

findings.title: predefined_task_recurring_rules.monthlyOrdinal and monthlyDayOfMonth have no range CHECK constraints despite documented semantics (1..5 and 1..31)
findings.code_evidence:
ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD COLUMN     "monthlyDayOfMonth" INTEGER,
ADD COLUMN     "monthlyOrdinal" INTEGER,
findings.description: The schema.prisma documents monthlyOrdinal as '1..5 (5 = dernière occurrence du mois) pour MONTHLY_ORDINAL' and monthlyDayOfMonth as '1..31 pour MONTHLY_DAY'. Neither column has a DB-level CHECK constraint anywhere in the migration history. An invalid monthlyDayOfMonth=32 or monthlyOrdinal=0 would be silently accepted, leading to recurrence rule evaluation errors at runtime (no matching day in month, undefined ordinal week). The business invariants migration (20260527120000) that fixed similar issues on weight, progress, allocation, etc. does not cover these fields.
findings.root_cause: The recurrence rule columns were added as raw INTEGER without accompanying CHECK constraints, and the subsequent DAT-003/DAT-004 cleanup missed them.
findings.impact: Invalid recurrence rules produce unpredictable task assignment behavior: monthlyDayOfMonth=32 generates no assignments or crashes the recurrence engine, monthlyOrdinal=6 may loop indefinitely or skip an entire month's assignments, corrupting the predefined task scheduling for HR teams.
findings.suggested_fix: ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD CONSTRAINT "ptrr_monthly_ordinal_ck" CHECK ("monthlyOrdinal" IS NULL OR ("monthlyOrdinal" BETWEEN 1 AND 5)); ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD CONSTRAINT "ptrr_monthly_day_of_month_ck" CHECK ("monthlyDayOfMonth" IS NULL OR ("monthlyDayOfMonth" BETWEEN 1 AND 31));
acceptance_criteria: INSERT with monthlyOrdinal=6 fails with check_violation
INSERT with monthlyDayOfMonth=32 fails with check_violation
INSERT with monthlyOrdinal=NULL succeeds (nullable column)
INSERT with monthlyDayOfMonth=31 succeeds
findings.verification_command: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM predefined_task_recurring_rules WHERE ("monthlyOrdinal" IS NOT NULL AND "monthlyOrdinal" NOT BETWEEN 1 AND 5) OR ("monthlyDayOfMonth" IS NOT NULL AND "monthlyDayOfMonth" NOT BETWEEN 1 AND 31);
findings.notes: Verified by exhaustive grep across all 19 scanned migrations and 15 additional CHECK-bearing migrations: no constraint added for these two fields anywhere.

findings.title: RBAC V4 drops role_permissions, permissions, role_configs tables and users.role column with no backup or preflight verification
findings.description: This migration irreversibly drops three tables (role_permissions, permissions, role_configs) and the users.role enum column, with only a comment advising to 'ensure a backup before prod deployment'. There is no preflight DO $$ ... RAISE EXCEPTION $$ block verifying that the V0 backfill was 100% complete (every user has a non-null roleId) before the legacy data is destroyed. Unlike the preceding V0 migration which has explicit post-backfill verification, V4 has none. If run on a database where V0's backfill had silently failed (e.g., a user with an unmapped legacy role left roleId NULL), the DROP destroys the only evidence of what roles existed.
findings.root_cause: The migration relies on a comment warning ('ensure backup') and the upstream V0 verification, but adds no self-contained guard to confirm the RBAC migration state before executing destructive DDL.
findings.impact: If V0's check was bypassed or the DB was in an intermediate state, V4 permanently destroys RBAC data for all users, leaving the application with no authorization data. Recovery requires a database backup. In production, this could mean a complete loss of all user role assignments.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a preflight DO block before the DROPs: DO $$ DECLARE null_count INTEGER; BEGIN SELECT COUNT(*) INTO null_count FROM users WHERE "roleId" IS NULL; IF null_count > 0 THEN RAISE EXCEPTION 'RBAC V4 preflight: % users still have NULL roleId — abort DROP', null_count; END IF; END $$;
acceptance_criteria: If any user has roleId IS NULL, the V4 migration fails with an informative exception
The migration succeeds only when all users have a valid roleId from V0 backfill
findings.verification_command: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE "roleId" IS NULL;
findings.notes: The V0 migration (20260419192835) has a correct post-backfill check at lines 100-107. V4 simply trusts that V0 ran and passed, which is not safe for migrations deployed independently or on partial database restores.

findings.title: predefined_task_recurring_rules lacks a XOR constraint ensuring exactly one of dayOfWeek (WEEKLY) or monthly fields (MONTHLY_DAY/MONTHLY_ORDINAL) is populated per recurrenceType
findings.code_evidence:
ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD COLUMN     "monthlyDayOfMonth" INTEGER,
ADD COLUMN     "monthlyOrdinal" INTEGER,
ADD COLUMN     "recurrenceType" TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'WEEKLY',
    ALTER COLUMN "dayOfWeek" DROP NOT NULL;
findings.description: After this migration, dayOfWeek becomes nullable and two new monthly fields are added. The schema comment says dayOfWeek is NULL for MONTHLY_DAY recurrenceType. But no CHECK constraint enforces the cross-column invariant: when recurrenceType='WEEKLY', dayOfWeek must be non-null and monthly fields must be null; when recurrenceType='MONTHLY_DAY', monthlyDayOfMonth must be non-null; when recurrenceType='MONTHLY_ORDINAL', monthlyOrdinal must be non-null. Without this constraint, it is possible to store recurrenceType='WEEKLY' with dayOfWeek=NULL (null WEEKLY rule), or recurrenceType='MONTHLY_DAY' with both monthlyDayOfMonth and monthlyOrdinal populated (ambiguous rule). No later migration adds this cross-column guard.
findings.root_cause: The migration drops NOT NULL on dayOfWeek and adds the monthly alternatives without a compensating CHECK constraint to ensure exactly the right fields are populated per recurrenceType.
findings.impact: Invalid recurrence rule combinations silently accepted at the DB layer produce incorrect or absent task assignments, causing HR teams to miss mandatory weekly predefined tasks or generate duplicate monthly assignments. Bugs are hard to diagnose because the data looks syntactically valid.
findings.suggested_fix: ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD CONSTRAINT "ptrr_recurrence_fields_xor_ck" CHECK ( ("recurrenceType" = 'WEEKLY' AND "dayOfWeek" IS NOT NULL AND "monthlyDayOfMonth" IS NULL AND "monthlyOrdinal" IS NULL) OR ("recurrenceType" = 'MONTHLY_DAY' AND "monthlyDayOfMonth" IS NOT NULL AND "dayOfWeek" IS NULL AND "monthlyOrdinal" IS NULL) OR ("recurrenceType" = 'MONTHLY_ORDINAL' AND "monthlyOrdinal" IS NOT NULL AND "dayOfWeek" IS NULL AND "monthlyDayOfMonth" IS NULL) );
acceptance_criteria: recurrenceType='WEEKLY' with dayOfWeek=NULL is rejected by check_violation
recurrenceType='MONTHLY_DAY' with both monthlyDayOfMonth and monthlyOrdinal populated is rejected
recurrenceType='WEEKLY' with dayOfWeek=1 and NULL monthly fields succeeds
findings.verification_command: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM predefined_task_recurring_rules WHERE ("recurrenceType" = 'WEEKLY' AND "dayOfWeek" IS NULL) OR ("recurrenceType" = 'MONTHLY_DAY' AND "monthlyDayOfMonth" IS NULL) OR ("recurrenceType" = 'MONTHLY_ORDINAL' AND "monthlyOrdinal" IS NULL);
findings.notes: The XOR pattern is already correctly implemented for time_entries actor fields (userId XOR thirdPartyId) in the same migration batch. The same pattern should apply to recurrenceType-conditional fields.

findings.title: predefined_tasks.startTime and endTime stored as TEXT with no format CHECK at column creation — open window before DAT-013
findings.description: startTime and endTime are added as unconstrained TEXT columns. The schema comments on these fields specify 'format HH:MM' but no CHECK constraint validates the format at creation time. The DAT-013 migration (20260527140000) later adds regex CHECK constraints for these columns, but that migration was applied 52 days later. During the window from 20260405 to 20260527, any string value could be inserted (e.g., '25:99', 'morning', ''). Pre-existing invalid values in production would cause DAT-013 to fail at deploy time with a check_violation when adding the constraint, revealing data quality issues that should have been prevented at write time.
findings.root_cause: The format constraint was not co-located with the column addition migration, creating a 52-day window where malformed time strings could enter the database.
findings.impact: Invalid time strings in predefined_tasks rows could cause task scheduling logic to fail silently or crash when parsing the time fields. The DAT-013 migration cannot be applied to a production database containing malformed values without first cleaning the data.
findings.suggested_fix: The column addition migration should have included: ADD CONSTRAINT "predefined_tasks_startTime_format_ck" CHECK ("startTime" ~ '^([0-1]?[0-9]|2[0-3]):[0-5][0-9]$'), ADD CONSTRAINT "predefined_tasks_endTime_format_ck" CHECK ("endTime" ~ '^([0-1]?[0-9]|2[0-3]):[0-5][0-9]$'). Before deploying DAT-013, verify: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM predefined_tasks WHERE "startTime" !~ '^([0-1]?[0-9]|2[0-3]):[0-5][0-9]$' OR "endTime" !~ '^([0-1]?[0-9]|2[0-3]):[0-5][0-9]$';
acceptance_criteria: No rows in predefined_tasks have startTime or endTime that do not match HH:MM format
The DAT-013 migration deploys without check_violation errors on production
findings.verification_command: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM predefined_tasks WHERE ("startTime" IS NOT NULL AND "startTime" !~ '^([0-1]?[0-9]|2[0-3]):[0-5][0-9]$') OR ("endTime" IS NOT NULL AND "endTime" !~ '^([0-1]?[0-9]|2[0-3]):[0-5][0-9]$');
findings.notes: Same pattern applies to tasks.startTime/endTime but those are outside this shard's scope.

findings.title: predefined_task_assignments.completionStatus and predefined_task_recurring_rules.recurrenceType added as TEXT (not enum) — open window for invalid values before DAT-012
findings.code_evidence:
ADD COLUMN     "completionStatus" TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'NOT_DONE',
ADD COLUMN     "notApplicableReason" TEXT;

-- AlterTable
ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD COLUMN     "monthlyDayOfMonth" INTEGER,
ADD COLUMN     "monthlyOrdinal" INTEGER,
ADD COLUMN     "recurrenceType" TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'WEEKLY',
findings.description: Both completionStatus and recurrenceType are added as unconstrained TEXT columns. The DAT-012 migration (20260527130000) promotes them to proper PostgreSQL enums (AssignmentCompletionStatus and RecurrenceType respectively). Between 20260424 and 20260527 (~33 days), any string value could be written to these columns. A misspelled status such as 'DONE ' (trailing space) or 'done' (lowercase) would be silently accepted but would break any application code doing equality checks. The conversion migration (USING (completionStatus::AssignmentCompletionStatus)) will fail if any non-enum-member value exists in production data.
findings.root_cause: Using TEXT for discriminator columns that have well-known enumerated values, without a CHECK constraint or immediate enum type, creates a data quality window.
findings.impact: Invalid completionStatus values may cause task assignment status queries to return incorrect counts, misrepresent team workload, and fail the DAT-012 enum promotion migration in production. Invalid recurrenceType values cause recurrence engine failures.
findings.suggested_fix: Before applying DAT-012 in production, run: SELECT DISTINCT "completionStatus" FROM predefined_task_assignments; SELECT DISTINCT "recurrenceType" FROM predefined_task_recurring_rules; — to confirm only valid values exist. Alternatively, add CHECK constraints immediately at column creation.
acceptance_criteria: SELECT DISTINCT completionStatus FROM predefined_task_assignments returns only {NOT_DONE, IN_PROGRESS, DONE, NOT_APPLICABLE}
SELECT DISTINCT recurrenceType FROM predefined_task_recurring_rules returns only {WEEKLY, MONTHLY_ORDINAL, MONTHLY_DAY}
findings.verification_command: SELECT DISTINCT "completionStatus" FROM predefined_task_assignments WHERE "completionStatus" NOT IN ('NOT_DONE','IN_PROGRESS','DONE','NOT_APPLICABLE');
findings.notes: DAT-012 already applied in production as of the audit date, so the conversion has likely succeeded. This is a design observation about the gap rather than a current production risk.

findings.title: clients.name has no unique constraint at creation time — duplicate client names possible during 35-day window before DAT-036
findings.description: The clients table is created with only a regular INDEX on name (not UNIQUE). The DAT-036 migration (20260528130000) later adds a UNIQUE index (clients_name_key) and drops the regular index — but that is 35 days later. During the window, duplicate client names could be inserted. The DAT-036 migration will fail at deploy time if duplicate names exist in production, requiring a deduplication step before the unique constraint can be applied.
findings.root_cause: The business uniqueness requirement for client names was not encoded in the schema at table creation time.
findings.impact: Duplicate clients with the same name create confusion in project-client linking and reporting. The DAT-036 migration cannot be applied without deduplication if duplicates exist in production.
findings.suggested_fix: Before applying DAT-036, verify uniqueness: SELECT name, COUNT(*) FROM clients GROUP BY name HAVING COUNT(*) > 1; — and deduplicate if needed. Future table creations should include UNIQUE constraints for business keys immediately.
acceptance_criteria: SELECT name, COUNT(*) FROM clients GROUP BY name HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 returns 0 rows
DAT-036 migration applies without error
findings.verification_command: SELECT name, COUNT(*) FROM clients GROUP BY name HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
findings.notes: DAT-036 (20260528130000) has already been applied. This is a historical gap. Current production state should be clean post-DAT-036 unless duplicates were inserted and survived the migration (which would mean DAT-036 failed, implying it was not applied cleanly).

findings.title: school_vacations has no endDate >= startDate CHECK at creation — guard added 48 days later in DAT-003
findings.code_evidence:
CREATE TABLE "school_vacations" (
    "id" TEXT NOT NULL,
    "name" VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    "startDate" DATE NOT NULL,
    "endDate" DATE NOT NULL,
    "zone" "SchoolVacationZone" NOT NULL,
    "year" INTEGER NOT NULL,
    "source" "SchoolVacationSource" NOT NULL DEFAULT 'MANUAL',
    "createdById" TEXT NOT NULL,
    "createdAt" TIMESTAMP(3) NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    "updatedAt" TIMESTAMP(3) NOT NULL,

    CONSTRAINT "school_vacations_pkey" PRIMARY KEY ("id")
);
findings.description: The school_vacations table is created without a CHECK constraint validating endDate >= startDate. This check is only added by DAT-003 (20260527120000), 48 days later. During the window, inverted date ranges (vacationEndDate < startDate) could be inserted, producing incorrect overlap calculations for leave management and task scheduling. An inverted school vacation range would corrupt any date-range query checking 'is this date within a school vacation period'.
findings.root_cause: Date-range ordering constraints are a cross-cutting concern that should be applied at table creation but were centralized into a single later migration (DAT-003).
findings.impact: Inverted school vacation date ranges silently stored in production produce incorrect results for any feature that checks whether a given date falls within a school vacation period (leave calendar display, telework planning, availability checks). Audit of pre-DAT-003 data is needed to confirm no invalid rows were inserted.
findings.suggested_fix: Verify: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM school_vacations WHERE "endDate" < "startDate"; — to confirm no inverted rows exist from the pre-DAT-003 window.
acceptance_criteria: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM school_vacations WHERE endDate < startDate returns 0
findings.notes: DAT-003 (20260527120000) confirmed to add school_vacations_dates_ck. This is a historical window issue, not a current schema gap.

verified_clean: 20260405120000_remove_started_status: TaskStatus enum replacement is correctly ordered (UPDATE data → DROP DEFAULT → ALTER TYPE → DROP OLD → RENAME); only tasks and projects.hiddenStatuses use the enum, both are updated; migration is effectively atomic under Prisma's implicit transaction wrapper.
20260405215351_add_external_intervention_to_predefined_tasks: simple boolean ADD COLUMN with NOT NULL DEFAULT false — idempotent, no data-loss risk.

20260411100717_add_third_parties_and_time_entry_actor_xor: XOR CHECK constraint (userId IS NOT NULL AND thirdPartyId IS NULL) OR (userId IS NULL AND thirdPartyId IS NOT NULL) is correctly defined; backfill of declaredById from userId is complete with post-backfill verification; migration wraps in explicit BEGIN/COMMIT; pre-checks prevent partial re-runs.
20260415131201_add_force_password_change: boolean ADD COLUMN with NOT NULL DEFAULT false — no data-loss, no cascade risk.

20260415135502_add_refresh_tokens: CASCADE on userId FK is correct (tokens should not outlive users); indexes on tokenHash (UNIQUE), userId, and expiresAt provide correct coverage for authentication and cleanup queries.

20260419192835_rbac_v0_add_roles_table: 26-role seed is idempotent via ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING; backfill is idempotent (WHERE roleId IS NULL); post-backfill check correctly rejects any user with unmapped legacy role; roleId nullable at this stage is intentional (becomes NOT NULL in V4).
20260421152235_add_timeentry_dismissal_and_composite_index: boolean ADD COLUMN, composite index creation — no data-loss, no cascade risk.
20260423075303_add_clients_module: clients and project_clients tables correctly use CASCADE on project_clients FK (join table semantics); clients.isActive soft-delete pattern present.
20260423175724_add_snapshot_analytics_fields: integer counter columns added with NOT NULL DEFAULT 0; composite index on (projectId, date) appropriate for time-series queries.

20260424111457_add_weight_and_audit_log: audit_logs CREATE TABLE structure is correct (JSONB payload, nullable actorId); indexes on (entityType, entityId) and (actorId, createdAt) are appropriate for lookup patterns.
20260427171000_add_predefined_task_telework_allowed: boolean ADD COLUMN with NOT NULL DEFAULT true — no data-loss.

20260506114304_project_archive: nullable archivedAt/archivedById columns correctly allow NULL for non-archived projects; SET NULL on archivedById FK is appropriate (user deletion should not cascade-delete project archive metadata).
20260523124537_drop_max_days_per_year: column drop is documented with Prisma warning comment; leave type configuration logic change is intentional per design.

20260523171000_self_approved_and_global_balance_unique: selfApproved boolean with DEFAULT false correctly identifies pre-existing rows as non-self-approved; partial UNIQUE INDEX on (leaveTypeId, year) WHERE userId IS NULL correctly handles PostgreSQL NULLS DISTINCT semantics for global balance rows.

20260524100000_dat005_backup_float_columns: backup tables use CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS and INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (id) DO NOTHING, making the migration idempotent; splitting into a separate migration from the conversion ensures the backup is committed before the ALTER runs; DOUBLE PRECISION is correct for a backup (preserving the original IEEE 754 representation for audit purposes).
